title: Judea Pearl on Why LLMs Won't “Scale” to AGI - YouTube
text:
It must be incredibly annoying for the leading 
thinkers of our generation to come out of the  
comfort of their study or lab into the 
world, sit down for a podcast interview,  
and then see their words formed into 
somebody’s tweet or LinkedIn post,  
conveying the idea exactly opposite to 
what they were saying in the interview.
The recent victim of this transfiguration 
was Judea Pearl, a Turing award laureate,  
UCLA professor, and the founding father 
of the branch of artificial intelligence  
focused on cause-and-effect world models.
In Sam Harris’s podcast, Judea Pearl simply 
said that the current architecture of  
the large-language models, or LLMs, 
based on the transformer algorithm,  
has mathematical limitations that do not allow 
the LLMs to internalize causal world models.  
Now, because humans, the only species exhibiting 
general intelligence at present, seems to rely on  
causal world models quite heavily for knowledge 
generalization, the working assumption is that  
the artificial general intelligence, or AGI, 
will have to rely on causal world models too.  
The observation that LLMs do not 
internalize causal world models  
efficiently leads Pearl to a conclusion that 
simple scaling of LLMs will not produce AGI.
Yet, somehow, the general public translates 
this reasoning into the conclusion that  
LLMs are a dead end. Harris actually asks Pearl 
whether LLMs are a dead end, and Pearl answers,  
no, they are a step towards it, but nobody 
seems to be paying attention by that point.
Let’s think about this for a second. It 
is simply not true that all OpenAI and its  
leading competitors do with the billions 
of dollars they spend on their models,  
is merely scaling their existing algorithms. 
The companies behind the frontier models  
may be greedy, duplicitous, or outright 
evil, but they are anything but stupid.
There is no engineer on Earth that built 
something successfully and then decided to  
build the same thing bigger—without trying to make 
a thousand tiny improvements in the new version,  
improvements that accumulate over time 
into transformative breakthroughs.  
Creative people are physically 
incapable of the kind of dumb  
scaling that the general public seems 
to attribute to OpenAI and its peers.
We have to agree that this idea of (quote) “simple 
scaling” is not something that happens in reality.  
We know that the starting point for OpenAI’s 
GPT-1 was the transformer algorithm invented by  
Google in 2017, but nobody honestly believes that 
today’s GPT5.3 is the same thing but bigger. It is  
a completely different beast I am sure, not only 
in its size but in its underlying mathematics too.
So now that we have established that the 
improvement in the modern LLMs is not only  
in scale but inevitably in their fundamentals,  
does one think that the evolving AI should 
implement the beautiful Pearlian mathematics  
of causal reasoning in order to become AGI? 
For all my long-standing personal admiration  
of the genius of Judea Pearl and his causal 
mathematics, I am not so sure, and here is why.
We only need to look at the history of the 
evolution of the AI natural language processing.  
Decades of research, hundreds of dissertations, 
and thousands of scientific papers dissecting  
the syntax and grammar and semantics of human 
languages, creating logically robust mathematical  
proofs erected an ideal upper limit of what a 
natural-language processing machine should be able  
to achieve at its peak. The relative simplicity 
of the transformer algorithm and the overwhelming  
acceptance by this civilization of the slop that 
it produces, so far away for the shining ideal of  
the formal natural language research, should be 
a sobering example, a bitter lesson, as it is.
Perlian mathematics of causality defines a similar 
shining ideal of what is ultimately possible  
for a thinking machine to achieve in rational 
reasoning under overwhelming uncertainty. But the  
elegance of the mathematical theory almost never 
translates into the simplicity in the real-world  
product engineering, and there is no reason to 
believe, that this time it will be different.
My bet on what is going to happen, just 
like Google’s transformer algorithm in 2017,  
some mathematician will stumble upon 
a relatively simple algorithm that  
will make LLMs produce causal reasoning slop 
that finally sounds better than the average  
5-year-old’s and the highly incentivized corporate 
executives will proclaim that ‘This Is AGI’.
